segments.title: Why the Little Boat Wars were more than motor-boat tactics
segments.summary: An introduction to the Mediterranean war fought by the SBS, commandos, submarines, and small raiding craft rather than a technical survey of motor torpedo boats.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke frames the subject as a wider campaign of maritime special operations, not simply the design history of motor torpedo boats and motor launches. The story links the Special Boat Service, Special Boat Section, commandos, and SAS through operations in the Second World War Mediterranean. Small teams used folding boats, submarines, requisitioned local craft, and larger raiding boats to reconnoitre coasts or strike targets. This opening is useful for understanding why the ‘Little Boat Wars’ are really about the organisation and employment of maritime special forces.

evidence.note: The opening defines the episode's scope as SBS, SAS, commando, and small-craft operations in the Second World War Mediterranean.

segments.title: Independent Companies and the Royal Marines' expeditionary role
segments.summary: The improvised Independent Companies drew on a ship-borne Royal Marines role but had to be expanded with Army manpower after Norway.
segments.body: The Independent Companies emerged from the search for irregular raiding options during the Norway crisis. Their detachments were expected to watch possible landing places and obtain local boats rather than operate as conventional infantry. Clarke argues that this resembled expeditionary tasks Royal Marines already performed from ships. Because the Corps lacked enough personnel for every wartime demand, the Army supplied much of the manpower and some companies were later absorbed into the developing commando system.

evidence.note: The lecture connects the Independent Companies to Norway, ship-based Royal Marines work, and the shortage of Royal Marines manpower.

segments.title: Why changing special-forces names complicate the story
segments.summary: The same raiders passed through overlapping organisations and labels, making unit names less reliable than the people and operations they describe.
segments.body: Clarke pauses over the difficulty of deciding whether a raid belongs to the Special Boat Section, Folding Boat Section, SAS, or another short-lived organisation. The men and their actions can often be identified more confidently than the administrative label attached to them at a particular moment. Units were formed, absorbed, renamed, and reorganised while the war was still under way. This warning helps viewers follow the later chronology without assuming that every use of ‘SBS’ refers to one continuous organisation.

evidence.note: Clarke explains that the raiders are identifiable but their shifting Special Boat, folding-boat, and SAS organisational labels are difficult to untangle.

segments.title: Roger Courtney and the Folding Boat Troop
segments.summary: Roger Courtney's experience with a folding kayak helped turn a personal skill into the small clandestine unit that became the SBS's ancestor.
segments.body: Clarke introduces Roger Courtney as an unassuming but unusually capable officer whose pre-war canoe journeys made a folding boat a practical war tool. Courtney had taken the folding kayak Buttercup down the Danube before bringing it into wartime service with his wife's blessing. He formed a twelve-man Folding Boat Troop around the ability to travel quietly from ship or submarine to shore. The episode shows how personal experience with a civilian craft could become the basis of a specialised maritime-raiding capability.

evidence.note: Clarke describes Courtney's canoe experience, Buttercup, and the formation of the twelve-man Folding Boat Troop.

segments.title: The Glengall infiltration demonstration
segments.summary: After arguments for his unit failed, Courtney used a folding boat to demonstrate how a raider could reach and mark a ship in harbour.
segments.body: Courtney first boarded the landing ship Glengall and returned with a gun cover and other evidence that showed how little notice a small craft could attract. Senior officers then challenged him to repeat the feat against a crew that knew he was coming. He reached the hull, chalked positions where limpet mines might have been placed, and even climbed an inviting rope before entering the captain's trap. Although he was caught, the demonstration proved the concept and secured twelve men and seven additional folding boats.

evidence.note: The account covers Courtney's two approaches to Glengall, the evidence he brought to the meeting, and the resulting expansion of his unit.

segments.title: Submarine-borne reconnaissance at Rhodes
segments.summary: The SBS used HMS Triumph and folding boats to collect the detailed beach intelligence that an ambitious Rhodes raid would have required.
segments.body: Sent to Egypt under Layforce, the SBS was meant to reconnoitre before commando attacks. Rhodes appeared lightly held, and British planners considered either a major raid or an attempt to seize the island, airfield, and harbour with naval gunfire behind the landing force. HMS Triumph carried Courtney, Nigel Willmott, and their folding boat close enough to survey beaches and navigation references over several nights. The final recovery nearly failed when Courtney suffered cramp, lost his signal torch, and was found by Willmott only minutes before an enemy patrol arrived.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the proposed Rhodes operation, the role of HMS Triumph, and the dangerous multi-night reconnaissance.

segments.title: Mount Etna railway sabotage from a submarine
segments.summary: A tiny SBS party used a submarine launch and a rail mine to disrupt a Sicilian line near Mount Etna, extending the striking power of HMS Urge.
segments.body: After reconnaissance through HMS Urge's periscope, the raiders landed near a railway tunnel beneath Mount Etna and slipped past fishermen on the beach. They scraped away earth by hand rather than risk the noise of a pickaxe, then fitted a contact mine to the rail. A train approached while they were still finishing the work, forcing them to withdraw without knowing whether the charge had functioned. Urge later raised its periscope in daylight and saw the damaged train and repair crews, confirming that a submarine could project striking power ashore beyond its torpedoes and deck gun.

evidence.note: Clarke recounts the rail-mine attack near Mount Etna, the uncertainty over its detonation, and the later sighting of repair activity.

segments.title: Small raids as a force multiplier
segments.summary: Minor acts of sabotage could impose disproportionate uncertainty, making British raiders look like a wider internal security problem.
segments.body: Clarke presents these attacks as carefully chosen, low-level disruptions that gave Malta-based submarines additional ways to hurt the enemy. A two-man landing party could sabotage infrastructure that the submarine could see but could not safely attack with its own weapons. The scattered incidents were difficult for defenders to attribute and forced them to consider threats along an extensive coastline. Italian authorities could therefore misread British landings as evidence of a partisan or communist problem, illustrating how tiny raids multiplied uncertainty as well as physical damage.

evidence.note: The lecture explains why apparently modest sabotage amplified the pressure exerted by Malta-based submarines.

segments.title: Courtney's standard for a quiet raider
segments.summary: Courtney sought men who could recognise an overlooked route, act quietly, and leave before the enemy understood what had happened.
segments.body: The desired raider was not an assault specialist looking for a dramatic frontal fight. Courtney wanted someone who would notice an unlocked side window rather than burst through the front door firing. That contrast makes stealth, judgement, and imagination more important than visible aggression. The goal was to complete the task so quietly that the enemy understood what had happened only after the raiders had gone.

evidence.note: Courtney's recruiting preference is described as finding men who identify and exploit an unobtrusive route into a target.

segments.title: Crete and the misuse of light forces
segments.summary: The battle of Crete shows why a lightly equipped raiding formation could not simply be used as a conventional front-line force.
segments.body: Layforce was committed to regular fighting at Crete even though it had been organised as a fast, lightly equipped raiding force. Clarke contrasts this with later commando formations that fought in the line only when backed by heavier weapons, fire support, and air power. Crete offered none of that coherence: senior-command failures and confusion afflicted both sides while German and Italian forces retained the advantage in the air. The Royal Navy's position worsened when HMS Formidable, its only available carrier with few aircraft, was damaged during an attempt to strike the principal Stuka base.

evidence.note: The lecture contrasts Layforce's intended role with its employment at Crete and discusses the lack of supporting combat power.

segments.title: Operation Album and the SBS–SAS divide
segments.summary: Joint attacks on airfields could succeed, but Operation Album also exposed the losses and practical differences between SBS and SAS methods.
segments.body: Operation Album paired the SBS with the SAS in attacks on four airfields and produced a substantial operational effect. It also cost the SBS many men, beginning a period of serious attrition. Clarke stresses that the organisations had been trained for different methods: SAS mobility suited desert airfield attacks, whereas folding-boat teams depended on discreet coastal insertion and recovery. David Stirling's successful and expanding SAS organisation nevertheless drew smaller special units into its orbit because it provided an established structure for special operations.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Operation Album, its losses, and the different operational styles of the SBS and SAS.

segments.title: 62 Commando and the small-scale raiding force
segments.summary: 62 Commando specialised in fast-boat raids, bringing a different scale of maritime raiding to operations such as Postmaster and Basalt.
segments.body: Where folding-boat teams emphasised covert approach, 62 Commando's Small Scale Raiding Force used high-speed boats for more substantial attacks. Clarke names operations Postmaster, Barricade, Riot, Acquaint, and Basalt to show the unit's breadth of work. Basalt also exposes the grim prisoner-control problem faced by a small raiding party after German captives tried to escape and were shot with their hands still tied. Every guard assigned to prisoners reduced the strength available for the raid, while limited boat capacity prevented the force from solving the problem by bringing many more men.

evidence.note: The segment identifies 62 Commando's boat-raiding role, named operations, and the constraints around guarding prisoners during a raid.

segments.title: Why paper strength overstates a raiding unit's power
segments.summary: A nominal establishment says little about how many raiders, berths, and boats are actually available after training injuries and operations.
segments.body: Clarke cautions against treating a commando's theoretical establishment of roughly 450 men as its usable strength. High-tempo training caused injuries, while combat added casualties, deaths, and the ordinary physical and mental wear of war. Men also needed rest or time away if the unit was to remain effective. Even after personnel availability was calculated, the number of boats and berths aboard the parent ship imposed a separate hard limit on the force that could actually be deployed.

evidence.note: The lecture contrasts nominal unit strength with losses, rest requirements, and the transport capacity available for an operation.

segments.title: Jellicoe and the re-formed SBS
segments.summary: After losses and reorganisation, the folding-boat section and 62 Commando became part of an SBS commanded by George Jellicoe.
segments.body: The original folding-boat force had been heavily depleted and drawn into the SAS system as Stirling's successful organisation absorbed smaller special units. Following David Stirling's capture, the formation was divided between the Special Raiding Squadron and a new SBS. That SBS combined the surviving SAS folding-boat element with 62 Commando. Lieutenant Colonel George Jellicoe then commanded a force capable of both clandestine small-boat work and larger maritime raids.

evidence.note: Clarke traces the attrition of the folding-boat section and describes the later SBS reorganisation under Jellicoe.

segments.title: Operation Angelo and the collapse of the independent folding-boat force
segments.summary: Operation Angelo achieved its immediate purpose but inflicted losses severe enough to end the folding-boat section as an independent formation.
segments.body: Operation Angelo illustrates the difference between tactical success and institutional survival. Clarke says that only David Sutherland and Duggan avoided capture and returned to the submarines, even though the operation itself achieved considerable success. The loss of the section's captain and so many trained raiders was devastating to a unit whose strength had already been falling. Its surviving organisation was consequently folded into the SAS Folding Boat Section rather than continuing independently.

evidence.note: Clarke describes Operation Angelo as operationally successful but devastating to the folding-boat formation, with only Sutherland and Duggan returning to the submarines.

segments.title: Operation Tenement and the limits of raiding claims
segments.summary: The raid on Symi shows how Jellicoe's SBS combined motor launches and local craft, while also inviting caution about claims of forces tied down.
segments.body: Clarke highlights Operation Tenement, the raid on Symi, as a larger maritime operation using ten motor launches alongside schooners and armed caïques. The mixture of craft let Jellicoe's SBS carry more combat power than a two-man folding-boat team and even defeat an encountered German E-boat. Raids across the Aegean, Adriatic, and nearby coasts forced defenders to cover many possible landing points, producing a claim that six German divisions were tied down. Clarke cautions that local partisans and SOE networks helped create this pressure, so the effect cannot be credited to the SBS alone.

evidence.note: The lecture describes Operation Tenement's mixed craft and qualifies broad claims about the number of German forces tied down.

segments.title: Recruiting for silence, judgement, and submarine discipline
segments.summary: Courtney's recruiting guidance valued the quiet, responsible operator who could protect a submarine and plan for every contingency.
segments.body: The recruitment standard rejects theatrical toughness in favour of someone quiet, responsible, and willing to let a completed mission be the only reward. Courtney warns that one careless act by two men in a cheap folding boat could expose a valuable submarine and its 52 trained crewmen. Raiders therefore had to understand naval rank, etiquette, secrecy, and the authority of the submarine's captain rather than behave as independent adventurers. They were also expected to check equipment personally, plan for contingencies, and protect scarce folding boats before leaving the parent submarine.

evidence.note: A contemporary recruitment document sets out the desired personal qualities, submarine discipline, and planning responsibilities.

segments.title: Why reconnaissance raiders and airfield attackers needed different methods
segments.summary: Two men landing from a submarine could scout or sabotage discreetly, but airfield attacks demanded more people, more firepower, and a different extraction plan.
segments.body: Clarke distinguishes the SBS model of a small team placing a charge and returning to its folding boat from SAS operations based on mobile desert columns. Vehicles made it sensible for the SAS to bring more men and enough explosives or firepower to attack an airfield, then escape by several possible routes. A foot party landed from a submarine had little support and had to return to one vulnerable beach and the same folding boats. Airfield attacks therefore became more practical when 62 Commando and the Small Scale Raiding Force supplied larger boats that could deliver sufficient mass and extract it quickly.

evidence.note: The lecture contrasts desert mobility with the fixed beach extraction problem faced by a submarine-launched folding-boat team.

segments.title: The postwar SBS niche: small teams rather than commando mass
segments.summary: Postwar SBS identity rested on tasks requiring a very small elite group, leaving larger commando assaults to Royal Marines formations.
segments.body: Clarke argues that the postwar SBS differentiated itself from wartime formations that had taken on larger raiding roles. Once a covert team is discovered and forced into a prolonged fight, the enemy can reinforce more easily than the raiders can. The Royal Marines already maintained commandos for assaults requiring mass, firepower, and larger transport. The SBS therefore grounded its distinct identity in Courtney's quiet reconnaissance and sabotage tradition: missions for which a very small elite group was more useful than a commando formation.

evidence.note: The conclusion contrasts the postwar SBS's small-team special-operations niche with the larger Royal Marines commando role.
